title: 5.1. Introduction
content:
5.1. Introduction This hands-on lab provides detailed guidelines on deploying the Resume Fit platform end-to-end on AWS. Resume Fit is an AI-powered web application evaluating compatibility score between Candidate Resumes (CVs) and Job Descriptions (JDs), aiming to facilitate efficient recruiting decisions.
The platform is designed using standard AWS reference architectures to guarantee high scalability and availability, including core components: virtual private network (VPC), elastic computing (EC2, Auto Scaling Groups), relational database (RDS PostgreSQL), object storage (S3), asynchronous message processing (SQS), system monitoring (CloudWatch), and security credentials management (Secrets Manager).

title: 5.3.1 Create VPC and Internet Gateway
content: 5.3.1. Create VPC and Internet Gateway Navigate to the VPC Console, click Create VPC. Name your VPC ResumeMatching-VPC and configure CIDR block 10.0.0.0/16. Enable DNS Hostnames on the VPC to resolve private AWS domains. Create an Internet Gateway named ResumeMatching-IGW to provide inbound/outbound connectivity to public-facing resources. Attach the Internet Gateway to your newly created VPC (ResumeMatching-VPC).

title: 5.4.1 Security Groups Creation
content: 5.4.1. Security Groups Creation ALB Security Group (ResumeMatching-ALB-SG): Open HTTP (80) and HTTPS (443) ports to 0.0.0.0/0. Backend Security Group (ResumeMatching-Backend-SG): Allow HTTP traffic on TCP, restricted to source matching ResumeMatching-ALB-SG. Worker Security Group (ResumeMatching-Worker-SG): Restricts incoming/outgoing traffic to the background queue worker instance. Database Security Group (ResumeMatching-RDS-SG): Open PostgreSQL port 5432 restricted to incoming connections originating from Backend and Worker Security Groups.

title: 5.5.1 S3 Bucket Initialization
content: 5.5.1. S3 Bucket Initialization In the S3 Console, click Create bucket to provision storage space for candidate CVs and JD requirements. Create a folder directory inside your S3 bucket. In the Permissions tab, edit the Cross-origin resource sharing (CORS) rule. Add JSON configurations authorizing GET, PUT, and POST methods to enable direct client uploads.

title: 5.6.1 Secrets Manager Configuration
content: 5.6.1. Secrets Manager Configuration In AWS Secrets Manager, create a new secret named ResumeMatching-Secrets to store sensitive application variables. Configure environment values including PostgreSQL database credentials, database endpoint, and OpenAI API Key.

title: 5.7.1 Launch EC2 Backend Instance
content: 5.7.1. Launch EC2 Backend Instance Generate a new RSA Key Pair (ResumeMatching-Key) for secure SSH terminal logins. In the EC2 Console, launch a t3.micro instance named ResumeMatching-Backend in the private app subnet.

title: 5.8.1 AMI Creation and Launch Template Setup
content: 5.8.1. AMI Creation and Launch Template Setup Capture the configured EC2 instance status by building an Amazon Machine Image (AMI) named ResumeMatching-Backend-AMI. Using this AMI, create a Launch Template named ResumeMatching-LT configuring network parameters, hardware specs, and security firewalls.

title: 5.9.1 Validate Auto Scaling Group Functionality
content: 5.9.1. Validate Auto Scaling Group Functionality Open the EC2 Console, navigate to the Instances list. Confirm the instances named ResumeMatching-ASG-Instance are running across multiple AZs. Legacy instances should be terminated as the ASG maintains system metrics configurations and cleans up unhealthy nodes.

title: Blog 1: System Monitoring on AWS
content:
SYSTEM MONITORING ON AWS: STREAMING CLOUDWATCH METRICS TO VPC-BASED OPENTELEMETRY COLLECTORS USING LAMBDA Managing observability for large-scale cloud deployments is always a challenging task, especially when trying to balance cost, flexibility, and security. Although OpenTelemetry is rapidly becoming the industry standard to prevent vendor lock-in, organizations often run into a significant technical hurdle: how to stream metrics from AWS CloudWatch directly into internal collectors isolated within a private VPC. To resolve this, using AWS Lambda as a bridging agent from CloudWatch Metric Streams to the internal network has proven highly effective, ensuring secure and real-time metrics delivery.
HERE ARE KEY HIGHLIGHTS OF THIS PUSH-BASED OBSERVABILITY ARCHITECTURE: Transition from Pull to Push Model: Utilizing tools like Prometheus to constantly poll data can cause API throttling, missed metrics, and increased API costs. The event-driven Push model via CloudWatch Metric Streams solves this, delivering metrics with sub-minute latency for real-time alerting. Overcoming Amazon Data Firehose Constraints: Although Firehose supports pushing data to HTTP endpoints, they are required to be public. To route data into private subnets, AWS deploys a Lambda Transform Function as a bridge. Firehose batches the data and invokes Lambda; the function then securely pushes the metrics through a Network Load Balancer (NLB) into the VPC. OpenTelemetry Collector at the Core: Running on Amazon EC2 instances inside the VPC, the Collector acts as the central processor with 3 flexible layers: Receivers (data ingestion), Processors (filtering, metadata enrichment, obfuscating sensitive data), and Exporters (sending data to final backends like Grafana, AWS X-Ray, or Amazon OpenSearch). Scalability and Fail-safe Backup: By fronting the EC2 collector instances with a Network Load Balancer (NLB), the TCP traffic is distributed evenly, allowing effortless horizontal scaling. Furthermore, an Amazon S3 bucket acts as a backup destination for Firehose (generating no storage costs if Lambda forwards the messages successfully). Cost Optimization and Vendor Independence: Embracing OpenTelemetry (Apache 2.0 licensed, free to use) eliminates software licensing fees for third-party platforms. Its standardized design allows teams to switch backend monitoring services in the future without re-engineering the telemetry pipeline. In conclusion, integrating AWS Lambda, Firehose, and NLB establishes a seamless bridge between public cloud metrics streams and closed private networks. This architecture resolves performance bottlenecks and API limits while giving teams absolute ownership of their metrics. By designing an intelligent, push-based data stream, enterprises can build a powerful, cost-optimized, and future-proof observability platform.
Solution Architecture Diagram Reference Links & Documentation Original Post on Facebook Group: Facebook Post LinkOfficial AWS Blog Guide: AWS Architecture Blog Guide

title: Event 1: FCAJ Community Day
content:
Harvest Report: “FCAJ Community Day” General Event Information Event Name: FCAJ Community Day Date: May 09, 2026 Location: 26th Floor, Bitexco Financial Tower, 02 Hai Trieu Street, Saigon Ward, Ho Chi Minh City Organizers: AWS Study Group in collaboration with the FCAJ Community Role: Attendee Event Objectives Program Goal: Create an environment and opportunities for young students (especially 3rd/4th-year students and fresh graduates) to share knowledge, practice presentation skills, build self-confidence in public speaking, and orient their career paths. Core Message: Methods for boosting learning motivation (dopamine hacking), Prompt Engineering optimization techniques, baseline mindset in the IT industry in the GenAI era, and standard pipelines for integrating AI in software engineering (the BMX method). Value for Attendees: Help participants overcome the fear of being replaced by AI, understand the extreme importance of computer science foundations, learn how to utilize AI tools appropriately, and form core professional qualities such as integrity and long-term thinking. Speaker List Speaker Long: Presented “Brain Hacking with Dopamine” to sustain consistent learning motivation. Speaker Thinh: Presented “Ultimate Prompt Engineering and AWS Architecture”. Speaker Khang: Solutions Architect at Cloud Kinetics. Speaker Thao: Software Developer at Vietnam International Bank (VIB). Key Highlights 1. Analysis of Current Realities Students and early professionals often lack long-term learning motivation and get easily distracted by social media because of the absence of “instant gratification” mechanisms. In work environments, relying heavily on AI tools for code generation without proper business context, system design documentation, and structural breakdowns results in buggy, unaligned codebases prone to AI hallucinations. 2. Solutions Presented For Effective Learning: Break down milestones, create short-term rewards, and build learning “streaks” (gamification) to trigger dopamine release in the brain. For Masterful AI Utilization: Focus heavily on foundational concepts. Define tasks using textual system specifications and descriptive documents (BMX workflow) before delegating code generation tasks to AI. 3. Technology, Services, and Tools Featured AWS Services: CloudFront, S3 (storage), Cognito (auth), API Gateway, Lambda (serverless backend), Bedrock (LLMs & Foundation Models), DynamoDB (NoSQL Database), CloudWatch (Monitoring). AI Tooling: Large Language Models (Claude, Gemini), “Promptimizer” browser extension, BMX framework. 4. Demos and Case Studies Showcased a production-grade system architecture on AWS combining various Serverless services and GenAI. Demoed the “Promptimizer” browser extension to highlight text and have AI optimize the prompt right on the screen. Core Takeaway: AI acts as an amplifier. If your software foundation is weak, AI will amplify those mistakes; if your foundation is strong, it will scale your productivity exponentially. Key Takeaways 1. Design Mindset Adopt a business-first approach. Always start from business domains, not the technologies. Develop a ubiquitous language between business stakeholders and technical developers to ensure alignment. 2. Technical Knowledge An effective prompt structure consists of: Role, Instruction, Context, Input, Output Format, and Constraints. 3. Best Practices in the AI Era Avoid context overflows by breaking down system inputs rather than dumping entire files. Leverage multi-agent workflows where AI agents play distinct roles (Product Manager, Architect, Developer, QA) to solve narrow development stories. 4. Real-world Professional Insights Hiring managers focus deeply on the candidate’s thought process and the justification behind architectural decisions rather than just correct outputs. Experience isn’t measured solely by years on the job, but by the diversity and depth of challenges resolved. Applying to Work Project Applications: Re-design software development workflows by investing time in comprehensive specs (VOD, System Architecture) first. Break projects into Epics and User Stories before feeding them to AI agents for implementation and automatic code review. Technologies to Experiment with: Leverage AWS Lambda and Amazon Cognito to construct serverless backends without managing underlying servers or user password databases. Deploy the “Promptimizer” extension to improve daily interactions with LLMs. Workflow Optimization: Apply the 2-minute rule: if a task takes less than 2 minutes to complete, do it immediately without delay. Event Experience Lessons from Speakers: Gained clarity on the 5 pillars of career success: Salary, Experience, Network, Knowledge, and Profile. Interactive Demos: The live demo of architecture designs and Promptimizer offered very practical and vivid visualizations of the concepts. Networking and Community: The FCAJ community cultivates a culture of encouraging sharing, ignoring the fear of making mistakes, and prioritizing teamwork to go far together. Most Impressive Concept: The emphasis on Integrity—being self-motivated to handle corner cases and perform tasks well even when no one is checking, and building long-term trust. Lessons Learned Crucial Takeaway: Master the foundations. Only when you understand the absolute core can you review and validate whether AI outputs are correct. Handling Mistakes: Failure is a necessary step of growth, especially for freshers. Organizations are willing to invest in your mistakes as long as you maintain a learning mindset. Future Development: Keep up with latest AWS updates to synthesize knowledge, with the goal of becoming a knowledge-sharer (speaker) rather than a passive content consumer. Event Photos Figure 1: Speaker discussing multi-agent AI development workflows
Figure 2: Slide on Growth Mindset principles
Figure 3: Presentation on the importance of Prompt Engineering
Figure 4: Group photo with the FCAJ community members

title: Week 1 Worklog
content: Week 1 Objectives: Understand the fundamentals of cloud computing (IaaS, PaaS, SaaS). Get familiar with AWS Global Infrastructure (Regions, AZs, Edge Locations). Set up and configure an AWS Free Tier account with baseline security. Tasks to be carried out this week: Day Task Start Date Completion Date Reference Material 2 - Learn overview of cloud computing, deployment models (Public, Private, Hybrid), and service models (IaaS, PaaS, SaaS). 20/04/2026 20/04/2026 What is Cloud Computing? - AWS3 - Study AWS Global Infrastructure: Regions, Availability Zones (AZs), Local Zones, and Edge Locations. 21/04/2026 21/04/2026 AWS Global Infrastructure Map4 - Register and configure AWS Free Tier account. Set up MFA for Root account, create budget alerts, and explore Billing Dashboard. 22/04/2026 22/04/2026 AWS Free Tier Details and Limits5 - Explore AWS Management Console interface. Learn how to search, navigate, and pin frequently used services. 23/04/2026 23/04/2026 AWS Overview Whitepaper6 - Study the Shared Responsibility Model and essential security concepts on AWS. 24/04/2026 24/04/2026 Shared Responsibility ModelWeek 1 Achievements: Gained clear understanding of Cloud Computing concepts and service types (IaaS, PaaS, SaaS). Successfully created and secured AWS Free Tier account with Multi-Factor Authentication (MFA). Mastered navigating the AWS Management Console and understood the significance of Regions and AZs.

title: Worklog
content:
This section outlines the detailed weekly worklog during my 12-week internship at Amazon Web Services (AWS) Vietnam. The bootcamp structure is designed to build a strong foundation in cloud computing and hands-on system deployment.
Below are the detailed weekly progress reports:
Week 1: Introduction to Cloud Computing and AWS Ecosystem (20/04/2026 - 24/04/2026)Week 2: Core AWS Services: EC2, S3, and IAM (29/04/2026 - 05/05/2026)Week 3: AWS Networking and Security: VPC, Subnets, and Routing (06/05/2026 - 12/05/2026)Week 4: EBS Storage, RDS Database, and CloudWatch Monitoring (13/05/2026 - 19/05/2026)Week 5: Elastic Load Balancing and Auto Scaling (20/05/2026 - 26/05/2026)Week 6: Serverless Computing: AWS Lambda and API Gateway (27/05/2026 - 02/06/2026)Week 7: Content Delivery & DNS: Route 53 and CloudFront CDN (03/06/2026 - 09/06/2026)Week 8: Infrastructure as Code (IaC) with AWS CloudFormation (10/06/2026 - 16/06/2026)Week 9: Advanced Security: AWS WAF, Shield, and Secrets Manager (17/06/2026 - 23/06/2026)Week 10: Containerization on AWS: Docker, ECS, and ECR (24/06/2026 - 28/06/2026)Week 11: System Synthesis, Integration, and Resource Optimization (01/07/2026 - 07/07/2026)Week 12: Final System Optimization, Review, and Report Submission (08/07/2026 - 11/07/2026)

title: 5.2. Prerequisites
content: 5.2. Prerequisites An AWS account with Administrator privileges. Basic IAM configuration experience to delegate permissions. Basic familiarity with Linux Shell and Docker.

title: 5.3.2 Subnet and Route Table Configurations
content:
5.3.2. Subnet and Route Table Configurations In the Subnets dashboard, create the following subnets using designated CIDR blocks:
Public Subnets: Public-Subnet-A (10.0.1.0/24), Public-Subnet-B (10.0.2.0/24). Private App Subnets: Private-App-A (10.0.11.0/24), Private-App-B (10.0.12.0/24). Private DB Subnets: Private-DB-A (10.0.21.0/24), Private-DB-B (10.0.22.0/24). Create a public Route Table named Public-RT in your VPC. In the Edit routes page of Public-RT, add a route pointing 0.0.0.0/0 to your Internet Gateway (ResumeMatching-IGW). Associate Public-RT with the two public subnets: Public-Subnet-A and Public-Subnet-B. Create dedicated Route Tables for App and DB layers, namely Private-App-RT (Subnet A), ResumeMatching-Private-App-RT-B (Subnet B), and Private-DB-RT (DB subnets), and associate them accordingly to prevent public visibility.

title: 5.4.2 IAM Roles Configuration
content: 5.4.2. IAM Roles Configuration In the IAM Console, create a role named ResumeMatching-Backend-Role to bind to the Backend EC2 instances. Create a second role named ResumeMatching-Worker-Role for the queue worker. Attach standard managed policies like AmazonSQSFullAccess and S3 bucket write/read access.

title: 5.5.2 SQS Queue Setup
content: 5.5.2. SQS Queue Setup Navigate to the SQS Console. Create a Standard queue named ResumeMatching-DLQ (Dead Letter Queue) to isolate failed message payloads. Create the main SQS queue named ResumeMatching-Queue.

title: 5.6.2 Database Instance Initialization
content: 5.6.2. Database Instance Initialization Navigate to the RDS Console, go to Subnet groups and create a group named resumematching-dbsubnetgroup containing Private-DB-A and Private-DB-B subnets. Create a new Database instance (DB identifier: resume-matching-db) running PostgreSQL engine on class db.t3.micro.

title: 5.7.2 Shell Access and Software Setup
content:
5.7.2. Shell Access and Software Setup Connect to the instance securely using Systems Manager Fleet Manager. Choose the running node and start a terminal session. Run commands to install Git:
sudo dnf install git Install and run Docker service to host container workloads:
sudo dnf install -y docker sudo systemctl enable docker sudo systemctl start docker Clone the application code repository under /opt directory:
cd /opt sudo git clone http://github.com/thuanthien-tran/resume-fit.git sudo chown -R ssm-user /opt/resume-fit

title: 5.8.2 Auto Scaling Group (ASG) Provisioning
content: 5.8.2. Auto Scaling Group (ASG) Provisioning Create a new Auto Scaling Group named ResumeMatching-ASG utilizing launch template ResumeMatching-LT. Set the Desired capacity to 2. The ASG will automatically scale out EC2 instances to meet application demands and provide high availability.

title: 5.9.2 Health Status Validation
content: 5.9.2. Health Status Validation Under Load Balancing, click Target Groups and choose the associated Target Group. Under the Targets tab, examine register details. Confirm the status shows a green Healthy tag, proving the web container is up and running.

title: Blog 2: Building an AI Gateway for Amazon Bedrock
content:
Hands-on Experience: Building an AI Gateway to Manage Amazon Bedrock with API Gateway Hi everyone! Recently, while working on AWS infrastructure labs—from configuring networks and troubleshooting security with Secrets Manager in a Cloud9 environment, to designing a proposal to integrate Machine Learning with Prometheus for system monitoring—I ran into a tough challenge: How do we manage access control and cost when multiple teams share Large Language Models (LLMs) on Amazon Bedrock?
In practice, when building Generative AI applications, you cannot simply hand out raw Bedrock API keys for client-side direct calls. Costs would skyrocket, and tenant isolation would be non-existent. After some research, I found an excellent reference pattern from Dynatrace and decided to test it. Today, I’ll share my experience designing an “AI Gateway” from a practical infrastructure perspective.
Solution Architecture: Lightweight Yet Powerful Instead of letting clients connect directly to Bedrock, I deployed an Amazon API Gateway as a central protective layer. The complete request flow is broken down as follows:
Route 53 (Optional but recommended): Instead of using the long default AWS endpoints, Route 53 maps a custom domain that fits enterprise styling. API Gateway (Main Entrance): Here I configured rate limiting rules to prevent spam and set usage quotas to stay within budget limits. Crucially, it supports response streaming, allowing real-time, token-by-token streaming from Bedrock models. Lambda Authorizer (Security Checkpoint): Requests are validated via a custom Lambda Authorizer verifying JWT tokens. Only authorized calls can proceed. Lambda Integration (Heart of the System): This Lambda function intercepts the client request (including headers, body, and query parameters), signs it using AWS Signature Version 4 (SigV4), and forwards it to the Amazon Bedrock API endpoint. Why Do I Recommend This Pattern? During my implementation, this pattern resolved two core problems that infrastructure engineers care about:
1. Transparent to Client I frequently test code locally using the Boto3 library. The beauty of this gateway is that client-side code requires almost no modification. It communicates seamlessly as if it was hitting Bedrock directly, while all control checks occur transparently behind the scenes.
2. Future-proof Design AWS continuously updates Bedrock with new models and features. If the gateway hardcoded specific API endpoints, every new model release would require code updates.
Since the Lambda Integration function simply packages and signs requests instead of parsing API models, the gateway remains completely unaffected when Bedrock introduces new models or updates features like Knowledge Bases.
Conclusion Coming from an observability background (working with Grafana or Zabbix), consolidating all AI traffic into a single API Gateway makes it easy to attach monitoring tools to track system health and project costs.
Leveraging Serverless services as a governance layer for GenAI applications not only improves security but also dramatically simplifies operations. If you are building GenAI infrastructure on AWS, this AI Gateway model is a must-have architectural pattern.
Solution Architecture Diagram Reference Links & Documentation Original Post on Facebook Group: Facebook Post LinkOfficial AWS Blog Guide: AWS Architecture Blog Guide

title: Event 2: FCAJ Community Day
content:
Harvest Report: “FCAJ Community Day (23-05-2026)” General Event Information Event Name: FCAJ Community Day Date: May 23, 2026 (Weekend Morning) Location: 36th Floor, Bitexco Financial Tower, 02 Hai Trieu Street, Saigon Ward, Ho Chi Minh City Organizers: AWS Study Group in collaboration with the FCAJ Community Role: Attendee Event Objectives Program Goal: Not just a standard technical seminar, the event serves as a platform for connection, networking, and mutual inspiration among IT professionals. Core Message: Covering themes ranging from AI and Cloud to real-world experiences from industry experts. Key discussions explored Prompt optimization, AWS services application (such as CloudFront), Hackathon journeys, and Enterprise-grade AI architecture design. Value for Attendees: Help participants understand job market dynamics under the influence of AI, prepare strong technical foundations to avoid obsolescence, and equip themselves with problem-solving mindsets for real-world enterprise scenarios. Speaker List Quynh Mai: Event MC and program host. Nguyen Gia Hung: Head of Solution Architecture at AWS Vietnam and Founder of FCAJ. Speaker 1 (Mr. Tinh): Platform Engineer at Gotam X. Speaker 2 (Ms. Hai Anh): Professional at Pacific Vietnam; previously presented at AWS Singapore Summit and in Silicon Valley. Speaker 3 (Mr. Thinh): DevOps Engineer. Speaker 4 (UTM Team): Developers sharing their Hackathon project experience. Speaker 5 (Mr. Duc): Technical sharing on Large Language Model Optimization (LLM Optimization). Speaker 6 (Ms. Vy): Professional at VPBank, sharing insights on Enterprise Multi-Agent systems. Key Highlights 1. Analysis of Current Realities New Job Demand: AI decreases software development costs, which in turn increases the aggregate demand for software products. This shifts job creation towards infrastructure management (Platform Engineering) and AI code auditing/debugging. AI Limitations: Generating code via AI without system architecture context leads to low-quality codebases prone to LLM hallucinations. 2. Solutions Presented Contextual Prompting: Always supply clear context (Context/System Prompts) using structure: Goal, Persona, Format instead of general open-ended questions. Multi-Agent Architectures: Implement Multi-Agent Systems to divide and conquer complex workflows (e.g., startup credit analysis) instead of routing all instructions to a single model. 3. Technology, Services, and Tools AWS Services: CloudFront (Flat-rate pricing, content compression, DDoS protection), AWS Bedrock, EC2, Lambda, VPC. AI/LLM: Amazon Agent platform, Obsidian (Second Brain knowledge management), Model Context Protocol (MCP). Infrastructure: Terraform (Infrastructure as Code - IaC). 4. Demos and Case Studies UTM Morpo Project: Demoed a tool using 3 AI agents to convert UI sketches into HTML code, allowing users to edit via drag-and-drop (a Hackathon winning project). LLM Parameter Demo: Compared LLMs set to Temperature = 0 running locally versus Bedrock on the Cloud. Proved that Cloud endpoints still show small variances due to backend inference optimizations. Enterprise Requirements: In enterprise settings, compliance, security, and guardrails take priority over the quantity of AI integrations. Key Takeaways 1. Design Mindset Remember that LLM is a Probabilistic Engine; do not assume it will always yield identical answers even with Temperature = 0. Do not leave systems on full auto-pilot; implement human oversight (Human in the loop). 2. Infrastructure Knowledge Learned how AWS CloudFront optimizes delivery costs through Flat-rate pricing, helping enterprises avoid billing shocks from traffic spikes or DDoS attacks. 3. Best Practices Avoid the “Internet Puller” syndrome (blindly copying online code snippets/tools without understanding their context and system compatibility). For Hackathons, tightly manage project scope and limit over-generation of ideas. Focus on solving a singular core problem within the 36-hour timeframe. Applying to Work Project Applications: Design multi-agent AI workflows assigning specific duties to dedicated agents (e.g., financial analyst agent, market researcher agent). Configure mTLS and VPC Origins for enhanced backend security. Technologies to Experiment with: Use Terraform for Infrastructure as Code (IaC) to manage environment versioning. Utilize AWS CloudFront edge security features (geo-blocking, bot blocking). Workflow Optimization: Automate meeting summaries and dashboard updates using AI Agents integrated with libraries (such as MCP connecting Excel) instead of manual data pulling. Event Experience Career Growth: Gained insights into the 5 pillars of hiring: Salary, Experience, Network, Knowledge, and Profile. Security awareness: Understood warnings regarding copy-pasting raw ChatGPT code directly into enterprise systems, which can compromise coding standards and open security vulnerabilities. Lessons Learned Foundational Knowledge: Core engineering concepts, university degrees, and Software Engineering basics remain essential. AI is a productivity multiplier, not a replacement for fundamental engineering skills. Business Justification: When pitching AI projects to leadership, always present the return on investment (ROI) and cost-savings statistics. System Integrity: When building systems, ensure that they are not only functional but also “secure, reliable, and serve the user’s actual needs”. Event Photos Figure 1: Speaker introducing the event agenda and session outlines
Figure 2: Slide on “Context is Everything - Making AI Actually Work for You”
Figure 3: Speaker sharing the 36-hour journey of building UTM Morpo
Figure 4: Group photo with experts and FCAJ community members

title: Proposal
content: AI Resume Matching & Interview Preparation Platform 1. Project Overview Goal: Web application evaluating the fit between CVs and JDs based on a standard 3-Tier AWS cloud infrastructure. AI Processing: An EC2 cluster (Backend + Worker) analyzes CVs and JDs by invoking OpenAI APIs over the Internet to optimize token consumption and save costs. 2. Cloud Infrastructure Architecture (AWS) Border & Global Layer: Content Delivery Network (CloudFront), WAF firewall, and authentication services are currently in a planned state (Planned but not deployed - AWS account verification required), they are not active services yet. Amazon S3 (Amazon Simple Storage Service) is used for general storage. Users interact via the Internet Gateway (1) or “upload via Pre-signed URL” directly to a dedicated S3 storage (S3 CV/JD Storage) (5). Network & Load Balancing (Inbound): A VPC with CIDR block 10.0.0.0/16 is used as the foundation. Internet Gateway (IGW) acts as the single gateway connecting to the Internet for both inbound and outbound traffic (via NAT Gateway). Application Load Balancer (ALB) is deployed across Availability Zones to receive traffic from the IGW and distribute it to the Auto Scaling Group. Application Layer: EC2 cluster (Backend + Worker) is placed in private app subnets (Private App Subnet A and Private App Subnet B), spanning two Availability Zones (AZ A and AZ B), and is managed by an Auto Scaling Group (ASG) to scale dynamically based on demand. Internal Communication: EC2 (AZ A) connects to the S3 storage service and SQS (6), followed by a Dead Letter Queue (DLQ). OpenAI API Flow: EC2 (AZ B) communicates outwards through the NAT Gateway in Public Subnet B to access the Internet Gateway. Outbound & Internal Network: Features two NAT Gateways, one in Public Subnet A and another in Public Subnet B, ensuring High Availability (HA) for outbound traffic from the Private Subnets. Communication to SQS and S3 is handled over the network, without utilizing VPC Endpoints in the current layout. Database Layer: Uses Amazon RDS PostgreSQL configured in Multi-AZ mode. Contains two Private DB Subnets (Private DB Subnet A and Private DB Subnet B) protected by a Security Group. RDS Primary (Master) is located in AZ A. RDS Standby Replica is located in AZ B with real-time synchronous replication (dashed line). Connection Flow: EC2 in AZ A connects directly to RDS Primary (7). EC2 in AZ B connects to RDS Standby Replica via RDS Endpoints (dashed line). Management & Monitoring: A Security & Management cluster (Vùng Bảo mật & Quản lý) includes AWS Systems Manager, Amazon CloudWatch (logs), and AWS Secrets Manager (API Key). Deployment: Source code repository hosted on GitHub (Manual Deployment). Automations such as AWS CodeBuild or AWS CodePipeline are not implemented in the current design. System Architecture Diagram

title: Week 2 Worklog
content: Week 2 Objectives: Master AWS IAM basics: Users, Groups, Policies, and Roles. Deploy and access Amazon EC2 instances via SSH. Configure Amazon S3 storage bucket and manage objects. Tasks to be carried out this week: Day Task Start Date Completion Date Reference Material 4 - Study AWS Identity and Access Management (IAM). Practice creating IAM Users, User Groups, Policies (JSON structure), and IAM Roles. 29/04/2026 29/04/2026 AWS IAM User Guide Introduction5 - Learn Amazon EC2 concepts: instance types, AMI (Amazon Machine Image), Security Groups, and Key Pairs. 30/04/2026 30/04/2026 AWS EC2 Concepts6 - Launch a Linux EC2 instance. Configure inbound Security Group rules. Connect to the instance using SSH from local machine. 01/05/2026 01/05/2026 Connecting to Linux Instance Guide2 - Study Amazon S3 (Simple Storage Service) storage classes (Standard, IA, Glacier), bucket policies, and Lifecycle Rules. 04/05/2026 04/05/2026 AWS S3 Intro Guide3 - Hands-on S3: Create S3 bucket, upload objects, configure public access settings, and test access via S3 object URLs. 05/05/2026 05/05/2026 Amazon S3 Getting StartedWeek 2 Achievements: Created non-root IAM admin users to follow security best practices. Successfully deployed an EC2 server and accessed it remotely via SSH. Learned S3 object storage management, permissions, and cost optimization via Lifecycle Policies.

title: 5.3. Build Virtual Networks
content: 5.3. Build Virtual Networks (Networking) A logically isolated network (VPC) provides a secure perimeter for your workloads, giving you complete control over routing and networking rules.

title: 5.3.3 Provision NAT Gateways for Private Subnets
content: 5.3.3. Provision NAT Gateways for Private Subnets Allocate two static Elastic IPs named ResumeMatching-EIP and ResumeMatching-EIP-B. Provision the first NAT Gateway named ResumeMatching-NAT inside Public-Subnet-A and associate it with the first Elastic IP. Provision the second NAT Gateway named ResumeMatching-NAT-B inside Public-Subnet-B and associate it with the second Elastic IP to ensure Availability Zone redundancy (High Availability).

title: 5.8.3 Target Group and Load Balancer Setup
content: 5.8.3. Target Group and Load Balancer Setup Create a new Target Group named ResumeMatching-TG choosing target type Instance routing HTTP requests on port 8080. Create an Application Load Balancer (ALB) named ResumeMatching-ALB. Configure it as an Internet-facing scheme, select two Availability Zones, and route traffic to the registered target group.

title: 5.9.3 CloudWatch Dashboard Analytics
content:
5.9.3. CloudWatch Dashboard Analytics Open CloudWatch and select the Metrics page.
EC2 Metrics: Access EC2 namespace and display CPUUtilization. Trace load fluctuations (e.g. 81.9%) during intensive document analysis. ALB Metrics: Search for RequestCount and TargetResponseTime to cross-examine traffic spikes against system latency.

title: Blog 3: Data Permissions with AWS Lake Formation
content:
A Handy Feature of AWS Lake Formation Recently, while researching Data Lakes on AWS, I came across an interesting article from AWS about Lake Formation and wanted to share it with everyone.
Looking at the architectural flow:
EMR Spark reads/writes data directly from S3 using IAM Permissions. Lake Formation manages access permissions on data tables in the Glue Catalog. Athena queries data using permissions defined in Lake Formation. This setups leads to a common situation: You can query data using Athena normally, but running Spark to directly access files in S3 triggers an Access Denied error. The reason is that Athena and Spark use two different authorization mechanisms:
Athena → Lake Formation Spark reading S3 files → IAM / S3 Policy AWS recently released a feature that addresses this challenge. Lake Formation can now grant temporary credentials so Spark can directly access data in S3 based on permissions configured in Lake Formation, via the API: GetTemporaryDataLocationCredentials().
Key Benefits of This Feature: Reduces overhead of managing permissions in multiple places. Minimizes Access Denied errors caused by mismatched configurations. Simplifies access for Spark, ETL, and Machine Learning workloads. Audits data access activities easily via CloudTrail. Important Technical Prerequisites: The S3 Location must be registered with Lake Formation. Requires Amazon EMR 6.15.0+ or 7.1.0+. Apache Iceberg is not yet supported. Cross-Region access is not yet supported. Overall, this is a highly useful upgrade for AWS Data Lake architectures, especially in environments combining both Athena and EMR Spark.
Solution Architecture Diagram Reference Links & Documentation Original Post on Facebook Group: Facebook Post LinkOfficial AWS Blog Guide: AWS Big Data Blog Guide

title: Blogs Posted
content:
This section aggregates the technical articles (Blogs) I have shared and posted on the AWS Study Groupcommunity:
Blog 1 - SYSTEM MONITORING ON AWS: STREAMING CLOUDWATCH METRICS TO VPC-BASED OPENTELEMETRY COLLECTORS USING LAMBDAThis blog post explores using AWS Lambda as a bridging agent to securely stream metrics from CloudWatch Metric Streams into an OpenTelemetry Collector isolated inside a private VPC subnet, achieving cost optimization and eliminating vendor lock-in.
Blog 2 - HANDS-ON EXPERIENCE: BUILDING AN AI GATEWAY TO MANAGE AMAZON BEDROCK WITH API GATEWAYThis blog post reviews designing and deploying an “AI Gateway” utilizing Amazon API Gateway and AWS Lambda to authenticate, rate limit, allocate quotas, and centrally govern access streams to Amazon Bedrock securely.
Blog 3 - A HANDY FEATURE OF AWS LAKE FORMATIONThis blog post reviews how AWS Lake Formation provisions temporary S3 data access credentials automatically for EMR Spark workloads, preventing access mismatches between Athena and Spark layers.

title: Event 3: FCAJ Community
content:
Harvest Report: “FCAJ Community” General Event Information Event Name: FCAJ Community Date: Academic Networking Event (Collaborated with HUTECH University and Swinburne University of Technology) Location: Bitexco Financial Tower (AWS Vietnam Office) Organizers: Team AWS G3 in collaboration with the First Cloud Journey Community Role: Attendee Objectives & Goals Share practical path guidance to becoming professional Cloud, Security, and DevOps Engineers. Introduce emerging technology trends including Machine Learning (ML), Docker, and GraphRAG artificial intelligence. Offer career orientation and discuss soft skills crucial for engineering teamwork efficiency. Speaker List Le Hoang Gia Dai: Senior at HUTECH University, core member of Team AWS G3. Bao Huynh: Junior Cloud Native Developer at Endava Vietnam and Founder/Head of ITea Lab. Tran Trung Vinh: System Administrator at Central Retail Group. Nguyen Quoc Bao: Technical Expert in Multiplayer Gaming and Cloud Computing. Truong Huy Phuoc: Professional Teamwork Skills Trainer. Viet Phat: AI Major Student at Swinburne University of Technology. Key Highlights 1. Network Security powered by Machine Learning Topic: WAF + ML for Cyber Attack Detection - Machine Learning-based Network Intrusion Detection System (NIDS) on AWS. Speaker: Le Hoang Gia Dai. Details: Discussed designing a Network Intrusion Detection System (NIDS) leveraging ML algorithms combined with AWS WAF to address limitations in traditional static rules. Used the academic CSE-CIC-IDS2018 dataset to train the model on real-world cyber threat patterns. 2. Containerization Technology with Docker Topic: Docker – A containerization technology. Speaker: Bao Huynh. Details: Shared details on Docker virtualization architecture, comparing VMs and Containers. Highlighted resources optimization, lightweight payloads, and the “Build once, run anywhere” philosophy. 3. Career Roadmap from Helpdesk to Sysadmin Speaker: Tran Trung Vinh. Details: Covered the career path from IT Helpdesk to Senior Sysadmin. Stressed that mastering Linux administration and Networking fundamentals is mandatory before advancing to DevOps or Cloud Architecture. 4. Multiplayer Game Development on Cloud Topic: Multiplayer in the Cloud - Connecting Godot Clients with AWS WebSockets. Speaker: Nguyen Quoc Bao. Details: Explored utilizing AWS WebSockets alongside AWS Lambda and DynamoDB to enable low-latency, real-time communication between multiple Godot game clients. 5. Next-generation Generative AI (GraphRAG) Topic: Build GraphRAG applications using Amazon Bedrock and Amazon Neptune. Speaker: Viet Phat. Details: Explained GraphRAG (Graph Retrieval-Augmented Generation) architectures utilizing Amazon Bedrock foundation models and Amazon Neptune graph database to optimize LLM lookup accuracy. 6. Efficient Teamwork Collaboration Speaker: Truong Huy Phuoc. Details: Shared 4 core rules for effective collaboration and introduced project management platforms like Trello and Slack to streamline developer pipelines. Key Takeaways 1. Design Mindset Cloud Mindset: Cloud computing is not just about renting servers, but designing auto-scaling, resilient architectures following the pay-as-you-go cost model. Data Integrity: Input data quality determines the final accuracy and detection rate of Machine Learning security models. 2. Technical Learnings Understood how to handle imbalanced datasets during ML model training. Acquired basic Docker CLI commands and serverless WebSockets connection flows on AWS. 3. Operational Best Practices Deployment Rule: Never test configuration parameters directly on a live Production environment to preserve system uptime. Automate repetitive workflows and document all configuration pipelines. Applying to Work Project Implementations: Containerize the AI Resume Matching application code using Docker to ensure local-to-production consistency. Bind AWS WAF to the ALB endpoints to safeguard against common web threats like SQL Injection and Cross-Site Scripting (XSS). Next Technologies to Experiment with: Test Amazon Bedrock API integrations to enhance the CV scanning and interview question generation accuracy of Resume Fit. Workflow Enhancements: Build real-time CloudWatch dashboards to increase threat visibility across networks. Event Experience Interactive Demos: Witnessed live demonstrations of NIDS threat detection, Docker containers in action, and real-time connectivity between Godot clients. Professional Networking: Interacted and networked via LinkedIn with senior speakers working at multinational enterprises (Endava, Central Retail). Most Impressive: The synergy between AWS services and ML/AI frameworks to solve complex, real-world security and operations problems. Lessons Learned ML for Threat Mitigation: Traditional signature-based security rules are inadequate against Zero-day threats; integrating ML is crucial to detect anomalous behaviors. Hands-on Project Priority: Your academic starting line is secondary to consistent hands-on lab practice and building physical portfolios rather than focusing solely on theoretical certifications. Career Focus: Master 1-2 core technical domains before expanding, and showcase your expertise through successfully completed projects. Event Photos Figure 1: Session explaining Docker containerization by speaker Bao Huynh
Figure 2: Session “WAF + ML for Cyber Attack Detection” by speaker Le Hoang Gia Dai
Figure 3: Session on Godot client communication via AWS WebSockets
Figure 4: Session on building GraphRAG applications utilizing Amazon Bedrock and Neptune

title: Self-Assessment
content:
During my internship at Amazon Web Services Viet Nam Company Limited from 20/04/2026 to 11/07/2026, I had the valuable opportunity to practice, learn, and apply the theoretical knowledge acquired in university to a real-world working environment.
I participated in the development of the AI Resume Matching & Interview Preparation Platform project, which enhanced my practical skills in setting up AWS infrastructure (EC2, S3, RDS, VPC) and integrating AI models.
Regarding work ethic, I always strived to complete my assigned tasks, respect my colleagues, and learn from senior members. Below is my objective self-assessment of my performance during the internship:
No. Criteria Description Good Fair Average 1 Professional knowledge & skills Understanding Cloud/AI services and applying them to the project ☐ ✅ ☐ 2 Ability to learn Absorbing new technologies (such as Multi-Agent systems, Terraform IaC) ☐ ✅ ☐ 3 Proactiveness Searching for documentation and researching solutions when facing errors ☐ ✅ ☐ 4 Sense of responsibility Completing assigned stories within the sprints ☐ ✅ ☐ 5 Discipline Adhering to schedules, reporting procedures, and workplace rules ☐ ☐ ✅ 6 Progressive mindset Actively listening to feedback from mentors and making improvements ☐ ✅ ☐ 7 Communication Presenting ideas and reporting blockers during daily stand-ups ☐ ☐ ✅ 8 Teamwork Coordinating effectively with other members of the development team ☐ ✅ ☐ 9 Professional conduct Maintaining a polite attitude and respecting company policies ☐ ✅ ☐ 10 Problem-solving skills Troubleshooting system issues (such as VPC network routing) ☐ ☐ ✅ 11 Contribution to project/team Coding backend features, writing tests, and drafting documentation ☐ ☐ ✅ 12 Overall General evaluation of the outcomes achieved during the internship ☐ ✅ ☐ Areas for Improvement Discipline: I need to build better time management habits to ensure punctuality and follow the daily progress reporting workflows more closely. Communication Skills: I should be more confident during team discussions and proactively raise technical blockers earlier instead of spending too much time searching for solutions on my own. Problem-solving Thinking: I need to improve my ability to troubleshoot system errors systematically, rather than relying on trial-and-error or copying snippets from the internet without fully understanding them.

title: Sharing and Feedback
content:
Below is my honest feedback and reflection after completing the 12-week journey of the First Cloud AI Journey (FCAJ) program in collaboration with AWS Vietnam:
Overall Evaluation 1. Learning & Working Environment
The learning and community environment at FCAJ is highly dynamic and inspiring. Attending in-person events at the AWS Vietnam office (26th & 36th Floor, Bitexco Financial Tower) allowed me to experience a professional corporate setting, which strongly motivated my career goals.
2. Support from Mentor & Organizers
The mentors (especially Mr. Gia Hung and the guest speakers at the events) are incredibly supportive and knowledgeable. Despite their busy schedules, they dedicated time to clarify technical issues and taught us the mindset to troubleshoot problems systematically rather than just giving away copy-paste answers.
3. Relevance of Work to Academic Major
As a student majoring in Cyber Security at HUTECH University, I was initially worried that Cloud/AI bootcamps might not align with my path. However, while building the AI Resume Matching Platform project, I was able to apply security principles firsthand by configuring mTLS, VPC Origins, setting up RDS Security Groups, and managing API keys securely using AWS Secrets Manager. This practical approach closely aligned with my major.
4. Learning & Skill Development Opportunities
The program provides a well-structured roadmap from basics to advanced levels (from AWS Free Tier, EC2, S3 up to advanced 3-Tier architectures, Terraform IaC, and GenAI Bedrock). I not only consolidated my technical skills but also learned how to write technical blogs, manage personal knowledge (Second Brain), and manage project scopes.
5. Community Culture & Team Spirit
FCAJ has a strong culture of sharing. The Facebook Group is active, where technical posts are reviewed quickly and receive high engagement. The Community Day events give students the opportunity to practice presenting their ideas confidently in front of a large audience.
6. Program Policies & Support for Members
The organizers prepared learning resources carefully, provided lovely souvenirs (swag) at in-person events, and sponsored AWS cloud credits to support students during hands-on lab sessions.
Feedback Questions What did you find most satisfying during your participation in the program?
It was the networking. I had the chance to meet and connect with leading experts at AWS Vietnam, as well as experienced Platform and DevOps Engineers. Additionally, building a complete 3-Tier Cloud application integrated with AI boosted my confidence in my engineering skills. What do you think the program should improve for future cohorts?
The volume of knowledge is quite high for a 12-week bootcamp (especially advanced topics like Multi-Agent systems or Terraform IaC in the final weeks). The organizers could allocate more practice time between weeks or offer additional online mini-tutoring sessions to assist students who require extra guidance. If recommending to a friend, would you suggest they join?
Absolutely. This is one of the few hands-on programs for students that brings high-quality practical knowledge, ensuring that we learn by doing rather than just studying dry theory. Suggestions & Expectations I hope the FCAJ community will continue hosting periodic Community Days to maintain a continuous learning network for future cohorts. In the future, I look forward to participating in the program as an assistant or mentor to guide the next generation of students based on the experiences I accumulated over the last 12 weeks.

title: 5.10. Verification and Testing
content:
5.10. Resume Fit Application Verification Access the ALB DNS address at /api/ready endpoint. Receive verification JSON payload: {"status": "ready"}. Load the Frontend UI. Go to the Register and Login windows, and authenticate. Execute the workflow: Create a Job Position (JD) and upload Candidate Resumes (CVs). Trigger evaluation. The platform uses OpenAI model APIs to return a candidate report including:
Match rating and overall suitability score. Gap analysis showing missing technical skillsets. Recommendations and automated generation of interview questions. You can also view assessment history, rank matching candidates, or check debugger logs.

Test and run containers locally to verify behavior. 25/06/2026 25/06/2026 Docker CLI Build Guide6 - Study Amazon Elastic Container Registry (ECR). Set up a repository and push the local Docker image using AWS CLI authentication. 26/06/2026 26/06/2026 Amazon ECR Guide6 - Learn Amazon ECS concepts (Clusters, Task Definitions, Services). Understand AWS Fargate serverless hosting for containers. 26/06/2026 26/06/2026 Amazon ECS Getting Started GuideWeek 10 Achievements: Created portable environment-agnostic deployment units with Docker. Deployed secure container storage workflows using AWS ECR. Understood container orchestration concepts on AWS ECS and AWS Fargate.

title: 5.11. Clean Up & Cost
content:
5.11. Clean Up & Cost After completing evaluations, clean up the resources to avoid active AWS subscription bills:
Delete the Auto Scaling Group, then terminate running EC2 instances. Delete the Application Load Balancer and its Target Group. Delete the RDS database instance (skip final snapshot creation) and delete the DB subnet group. Delete the NAT Gateways. Once deleted, release the allocated Elastic IPs. Delete VPC endpoints, route tables, subnets, and the main VPC. Empty all S3 bucket files, and delete the bucket. 3. Cost Breakdown The following estimate is prepared in the style of an AWS cost report, using AWS Pricing Calculator assumptions for US East (N. Virginia) - us-east-1, On-Demand pricing, and 730 hours/month.
STT AWS Service Configuration Quantity Estimated Monthly Cost (USD) Notes Total (USD) 1 Amazon VPC 01 VPC, 02 public subnets, 02 private application subnets, 02 private database subnets, 01 Internet Gateway, route tables, and Security Groups 1 set 0.000 Network foundation only; no direct hourly charge 0.000 2 Internet Gateway 01 Internet Gateway attached to the VPC 1 0.000 No direct charge 0.000 3 Public / Private Subnets 06 subnets in total 6 0.000 No direct charge 0.000 4 Security Group Security rules for EC2, ALB, and RDS access control 1 set 0.000 No direct charge 0.000 5 NAT Gateway 02 NAT Gateways in us-east-1 2 32.963 Approx. USD 0.045/hour per NAT Gateway x 730 hours + low data processing 65.925 6 Amazon EC2 t3.micro Linux instance for backend 3 7.592 Approx. USD 0.0104/hour per instance x 730 hours 22.776 7 Amazon EBS gp3, 30 GB per EC2 instance 3 volumes 2.400 30 GB x 3 instances x USD 0.08/GB-month 7.200 8 Amazon Machine Image (AMI) AMI snapshot storage for one 30 GB image 1 1.500 Snapshot-based AMI storage estimate 1.500 9 Launch Template EC2 launch template for Auto Scaling deployment 1 0.000 Configuration object only 0.000 10 Auto Scaling Group 02 instances in ASG 1 set 0.000 Management layer only; EC2 cost is counted separately 0.000 11 Application Load Balancer 01 ALB in us-east-1 1 22.265 Approx. USD 0.0225/hour + light LCU usage 22.265 12 Target Group 01 target group for ALB forwarding 1 0.000 No direct charge 0.000 13 Amazon RDS db.t3.micro, Single-AZ, PostgreSQL, 20 GB gp3 storage 1 15.440 Approx. USD 0.018/hour + 20 GB storage 15.440 14 Amazon S3 Standard storage, approximately 5 GB 1 bucket 0.115 Storage only; request volume is assumed minimal 0.115 15 Amazon SQS Standard Queue + DLQ 2 queues 0.000 Low demo traffic remains within free tier threshold 0.000 16 AWS Secrets Manager 01 secret 1 0.400 USD 0.40 per secret-month 0.400 17 Amazon Cognito User authentication below 50,000 MAU 1 user pool 0.000 Demo usage assumed to remain within free tier 0.000 18 AWS Systems Manager Session Manager for instance access 1 0.000 No direct charge for Session Manager usage 0.000 19 Amazon CloudWatch Basic metrics and logs 1 set 0.530 Light log ingestion and retention for demo monitoring 0.530 Total estimated monthly cost: USD 136.151
Cost analysis Highest-cost service: NAT Gateway, with an estimated monthly cost of USD 65.925, accounting for approximately 48.42% of the total monthly cost. Other significant cost drivers: Application Load Balancer (USD 22.265) and EC2 instances (USD 22.776 combined) are the next largest components. AWS Credits assessment: The estimated monthly cost is below the USD 200 AWS Credits limit. Therefore, the current architecture is financially feasible for the demo and thesis defense scope. Cost optimization recommendations Reduce NAT Gateway usage where possible, because it is the dominant cost component. Delete non-production resources immediately after evaluation, especially NAT Gateways, RDS, and ALB. Use the smallest suitable instance type already adopted in the current design, and avoid unnecessary scaling beyond the demo requirement. Apply short log retention in CloudWatch and keep S3 storage limited to essential artifacts only. Preserve the current architecture but schedule cleanup promptly after each demonstration session to avoid idle charges.

title: Week 11 Worklog
content: Week 11 Objectives: Review and synthesize all learned AWS core services and architectures. Deploy a comprehensive 3-tier architecture or full-stack solution on AWS. Perform security audits and network connection validation checks. Analyze and optimize resources using cost and performance tools. Tasks to be carried out this week: Day Task Start Date Completion Date Reference Material 4 - Review and synthesize AWS architectures (compute, database, networking, security). Address gaps in knowledge. 01/07/2026 01/07/2026 5 - Deploy a complete multi-tier web application (VPC, Public/Private Subnets, EC2 Web Servers under ALB, Multi-AZ RDS, and S3). 02/07/2026 02/07/2026 6 - Verify security boundaries. Implement restrictive Security Group rules. Test traffic flow between public and private layers. 03/07/2026 03/07/2026 2 - Conduct load testing on the deployed system. Monitor behaviors using CloudWatch dashboard metrics. 06/07/2026 06/07/2026 3 - Evaluate resources using AWS Trusted Advisor and AWS Budgets to find cost saving and optimization opportunities. 07/07/2026 07/07/2026 Week 11 Achievements: Successfully integrated multiple AWS components into a secure, scalable web architecture. Validated secure connectivity ensuring backend components remain isolated from direct public internet access. Identified and terminated unused resources, achieving cost savings while maintaining performance metrics.

title: Week 12 Worklog
content: Week 12 Objectives: Finalize the production-ready AWS deployment architecture. Verify backup, recovery, and cleanup procedures. Summarize knowledge, achievements, and lessons learned from the internship. Complete and submit the final internship report and prepare presentation slides. Tasks to be carried out this week: Day Task Start Date Completion Date Reference Material 4 - Finalize system endpoints. Clean up mock logs and implement final security refinements. 08/07/2026 08/07/2026 5 - Confirm backups are operational. Delete temporary stacks and idle instances to prevent future billing charges. 09/07/2026 09/07/2026 6 - Summarize core internship findings, AWS skills acquired, and document team contributions. Complete report write-ups and slides. 10/07/2026 10/07/2026 Week 12 Achievements: Finalized the full AWS system structure adhering to the Well-Architected Framework guidelines. Successfully consolidated 12 weeks of technical learning, blogs, and workshops into the final repository. Completed the final internship report draft ready for review by instructors and mentors.
